text.title: Build vs Buy for AI Features
text.summary: Decision rules for building vs buying AI capabilities, EU AI Act compliance framing, and vendor risk management.

rule.statement: Build when the feature is a core differentiator AND you have a unique data advantage. Buy otherwise.
rule.rationale: Building a commodity AI feature (e.g., standard text classification) consumes engineering resources without creating a moat. Buying a feature that is core to your differentiation cedes control of your competitive advantage to a vendor.

list.item: Build: core differentiator + unique data advantage + long-term competitive moat.
Buy (now) → Build (later): core differentiator but data not yet mature enough to support custom model.
Buy: commodity AI feature + speed-to-market priority + no data advantage.

rule.statement: Revisit build-vs-buy decisions at every planning cycle (at minimum annually).
rule.rationale: The AI vendor landscape changes fast enough to invalidate a 12-month-old decision. Capabilities that required building a year ago may now be available from a vendor at 1/10th the cost.

rule.statement: Document the vendor risk and fallback plan for every bought AI capability before it enters production.
rule.rationale: API deprecation, price changes, and capability regressions can all break an AI-native product without a code change. Without a documented fallback, the product has an undisclosed single point of failure.

rule.statement: Classify every AI component by EU AI Act risk tier (unacceptable / high / limited / minimal) and document applicable obligations before launch.
rule.rationale: Compliance requirements vary by risk tier. High-risk AI systems require documentation, transparency measures, human oversight, and bias testing. Discovering obligations post-launch forces expensive retrofits.

rule.statement: Agent-generated EU AI Act obligation checklists are starting points, not legal advice. Legal counsel review is required for any product in a regulated domain before public launch.
rule.rationale: Implementing acts change specific obligations over time. Regulatory accountability requires a qualified legal review, not just an LLM-generated checklist.

antipattern.description: Deferring bias testing to post-launch because "the model provider handles fairness."
antipattern.reason: The product owner is responsible for AI outcomes in the product context, regardless of the model provider's general fairness work. Bias in your specific use case and data distribution is your obligation to assess.

text.reference: AIFeature dataclass with build/buy decision logic
